author: @aaronfranklin324
text:
Every LED street light, every home modem every 5G cellphone.... Is a basestation in the network. 
If you leave your home modem or digital TV switched on its using some 200W to pass on other people's data. 
Things turned into a warzone everywhere they installed them in urban areas in New Zealand a few Years ago. 
I had to evacuate to an area not saturated. Can't sleep or concentrate around them. 
Official medical diagnosis as "electrosensitive".
Though it negatively affects everyone, testing has proven, some notice it more than others.
Those of us capable of objective observation. 🙄

author: @jamest.5001
text: Take a few fiberglass driveway reflector rods,( or bamboo) mount the bottom solid to the ground,.  Use a couple u-bolts to connect it to a bit of flat bar, 1.5"-3" depending on length, and size of bar,  basically building a large flex beam TQ wrench,  with a large feather on the top! The 12"-24" of flat bar with a flat sheet of fiberglass board, steel sheet, but maybe not so efficient,  form a inverted triangle as wide as the resonant frequency allows it to travel, likely 12", 6" on each side of the stationary beam, mount another inverted triangle,  mount rows of alternating 1"x. 125" magnets on the moving triangle, a row of 12 at the top drop down 1.5", cand add a row of 10, drop down, add a row of 8, then a row of 6, then 4, then 3 magnets, with either a coil per row, of 18 gauge wire, on a. 1" bobbin,. And .75" thick , coil until. 75" off the bobbin,  step down and add another,  wire to a rectifier then series/ parallel to get desired voltage, maybe go to 20-22 gauge wire,   basically make a large feather   about 6' tall get it to blow backward and forward atleast 1",2" total a rough guess  it should do 5 amps atleast depending on series or parallel series, have 6 coils to allow it to operate as two 3phase generators,  but two 2phase would work as well!! With the leaf or feather, flapping about, making power 🎉

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text: But the whole point of wind is that it is mostly "directional" for a period of time. A windmill will gather much more energy than a vibrational harvester using off the shelf components. Honestly, I don't think this one is worth your efforts. I know I know... but, really... for the amount of effort, the generation will be extremely low. I think I have said this before, but, why bother making these when 1 small solar panel will do much better, and at a lower cost, probably by a factor of 10 or more, if you take into account time and materials. This method requires "vibration", and basically most of the energy is wasted passing around the harvester.  You'd also be better off with a windmill alone, rather than add vibrational generation... unless there's some new miracle device that can efficiently do vibration generation that comes along. You can prove this to yourself... Compare 1 cubic meter of space using harvester generation vs 1 cubic meter of Wind or Solar.... There's no way the harvester will win using current generation off the shelf tech. Yes, we are "wasting" batteries, but we will be wasting much more than batteries by building fields of nano-generators that cost as much as a battery per device and don't deliver any real usable electricity vs Solar and Wind for the same space.

author: @markstudden9090
text: I remember seeing something similar having been done at a tree level. IIRC a bunch of closely spaced pine trees in a forest had been fitted with wires that ran from each of their trunks - a metre or two above ground level - to a central point, where the pulling force in constantly altering directions was somehow harnessed to something like a dynamo - the mass of the trees pulling in a common direction at any one point meant that the gearing to the dynamo could exploit quite a lot of torque, and spin the thing pretty fast. I think this was being experimented with in a Scandi country? Any idea where those investigations went - is it still a tech that's being used, or was it a dead-end?

author: @mouse_mc
text: Loving the new scenery. Also the micro generation idea. With all our loadshedding getting ridiculous here in South Africa, I am considering a noisy fuel generator like my neighbour or an expensive power bank thingy. But, I already have some solar lights and other rechargable lights, so am strongly considering individual power sources for things like: WiFi router, TV, fridge. Gas for heating and so on. But all the ideas are great, everything has its place. You have inspired me to take out my soldering iron, scrap batteries and even planning on buying that cheap charging board soon. Already made my first rechargable solar light from various old parts just lying around. Oh and big Clive helps too.

author: @NeoShameMan
text: Regarding this video, I found that a lot of comfort stuff use surprisingly very little energy, my computer use 60W I need to run it 10h and it still doesn't consume as much as my 700W rice cooker! but these small energy device are luxuries, I need to make food and the most energy goes to essentially boiling water, and that's survival! Basically we NEED 1kWh just to survive! and the other thing that consume the most energy all boil water in some form, such as the washing machine! But water being such a simple element, it mean we have a cap on how much energy we need. the 1 kWh is a symbolic threshold for basic survival, anything else is luxuries!

replies.author: @ElizabethGreene

author: @NeoShameMan
text:
Another non sequitr, I'm sorry, I have so many video to binge back, lol BUT Alberta university has apparently discovered a powder mixture that produce hydrogen with any water at ambient temperature, through a catalytic reactions with no energy input, but they won't tell us what's in the mixture except it's safe for human and environement!
I ask my friend Bing Chat to reduce candidate based on these information, and after eliminating unsafe elements, what remained was calcium, and the catalyst could iron, nickel, platinum and carbonic anhydrase, there is probably more, but that's seems more doable by me than an SOEC!
The researcher discovered the mixture by accident after leaving the mixture over night, they working on hydrogen fuel cell, and their plan is to use hydrogen fuel cell with their discovery to purify water all while generating electricity. Given what they say, it seems to me the split oxygen is captured by the process.

author: @55Ramius
text: I have always wondered how so many small generators can be linked to provide more power but not interfere with each other. Like power from one traveling out but backtracking into the next one. Feedback kind of thing.

replies.author: @TheWingnut58
replies.text: Fairly simple actually....use diodes to restrict current flow direction and wire them in series to bring voltage to desired level, then wire those "sets" in parallel to raise current to desired level......"in a nutshell"

replies.author: @pjarma8536
replies.text: Are you talking about perpetual energy device?

replies.author: @55Ramius
replies.text: @pjarma8536 Me?  No.. It is just that if you come up with something that does generate some power but not enough to hardly light a led, then having many of such devices would make it more able to do real work. I was just wondering how a person would connect all of them without one feeding back on the others. That was answered by a person already though. As for perpetual devises, I think many are on the right track to make something that can run much longer than normal but not reach over-unity. I still believe in the law of physics but still have an interest in things people come up with. Not those stupid combinations like taping a battery to a magnet and running a bulb, etc.

replies.author: @pjarma8536
replies.text: @55Ramius oh ic I could of answered that too you use what’s called a parallel circuit where you join all the lives in a row and all the negatives in a row.
